title: Opening Statement to the House of Representatives Standing Committee on Economics
summary: RBA Governor Michele Bullock testified before the House of Representatives Standing Committee on Economics, confirming the Monetary Policy Board's decision earlier that week to raise the cash rate target by 25 basis points to 3.85%, citing persistent inflation pressures. Underlying inflation rose to 3.4% in Q4 2025 from 2.7% in mid-2025, with the RBA now projecting inflation won't return to the 2-3% target band until mid-2027. The Board cited stronger-than-expected domestic demand, resilient global growth, and easing financial conditions as factors driving the inflation pickup.
key_points: RBA raised cash rate target by 25 basis points to 3.85% at its February 2026 meeting, the first rate increase in the current cycle
Underlying (trimmed mean) inflation increased from 2.7% in mid-2025 to 3.4% over the year to December quarter 2025; headline inflation reached 3.6%
Unemployment rate averaged 4.2% in December quarter 2025, described as low by historical standards
The RBA now forecasts inflation won't return to the 2-3% target band until mid-2027, a significant revision from August 2025 forecasts
Key drivers of inflation pickup: easing financial conditions, stronger global growth from AI investment boom, stronger private demand (household consumption and business investment)
The Board judged that excess demand is pushing against the economy's supply capacity, requiring tighter monetary policy
why_it_matters: The RBA's pivot to rate hikes marks a significant shift in monetary policy stance, signaling concern that inflation has become more persistent than previously anticipated. For Australian mortgage holders, this means higher borrowing costs. For investors, the RBA's revised timeline for returning to target (mid-2027) suggests a prolonged period of elevated rates. The testimony also provides insight into the RBA's assessment of global economic resilience and the role of AI investment in driving demand.
macro_context: The RBA's decision reflects a global trend of central banks reassessing inflation persistence. The testimony highlights that global trading system adjusted faster than expected to tariff changes, and AI investment has spilled over to high-income Asian economies supplying chips and key inputs. Domestically, both household consumption and business investment drove stronger-than-expected demand. The RBA explicitly noted it will monitor whether the inflation pickup is temporary or persistent, with risks skewed toward further capacity pressures if demand remains strong.

issues_found: Key point states this was 'the first rate increase in the current cycle' but the source does not explicitly state this. The source only says 'Since we last met with the Committee, the Monetary Policy Board judged that it was appropriate to tighten monetary policy.' This characterization is inferred but not directly supported by the source text.

The why_it_matters section states 'rates will remain elevated longer than market expectations had priced in' but the source does not discuss what market expectations were or what markets had priced in. This is a reasonable inference but not directly supported by the source text.

notes: Source is a transcript of RBA Governor's congressional testimony, classified as a speech for source_type purposes. The speech confirms a rate decision made earlier in the week (Feb 3-4, 2026 board meeting). Source is recent (Feb 6, 2026) and highly relevant to macro catalysts. The source body was truncated at the end, but the key policy content is captured. No explicit market reaction commentary is provided in the source.
